food_subgroup: Leafy green vegetables with sauce dressing

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a spinach food product class but no specific entry for sesame-dressed spinach; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers spinach as a raw commodity; no specific code exists for sesame-dressed cooked spinach preparation. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 256319004 identifies spinach as a substance; no specific concept exists for the prepared dish with sesame sauce. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of spinach with Beijing sesame sauce is approximately 150 g, typically presented as a cold appetiser plate of blanched spinach dressed with sesame paste sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Spinach, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt (USDA SR Legacy #11458) combined with Sesame butter, paste (USDA SR Legacy #12166), proportionally weighted for a typical Beijing sesame-dressed spinach preparation (approximately 80% spinach, 20% sesame paste dressing components)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for 老北京麻酱菠菜 exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values estimated by proportional combination: approximately 80 g cooked spinach per 100 g dish (USDA SR Legacy #11458: energy 23 kcal, protein 3.0 g, fat 0.4 g, carbohydrate 3.8 g per 100 g) and approximately 20 g sesame paste contribution per 100 g dish (USDA SR Legacy #12166: energy 586 kcal, protein 17.8 g, fat 53.8 g, carbohydrate 21.2 g per 100 g), plus minor soy sauce, garlic, sesame oil, vinegar, sugar contributions. Weighted composite yields approximately: energy ~136 kcal, protein ~5.9 g, fat ~11.1 g, carbohydrate ~7.3 g per 100 g. Sodium estimated from soy sauce contribution (~300 mg per 100 g dish). Values are estimates; confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; cooked spinach and sesame paste analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 老北京麻酱菠菜. GI estimated at 15 based on: (1) cooked spinach GI ~15 (low-GI leafy vegetable, University of Sydney GI Database); (2) sesame paste has negligible glycemic impact due to high fat and protein content suppressing glucose response. The dish is predominantly spinach with a high-fat sesame dressing, resulting in a low overall GI. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (15 × 7.3 × 1.5) / 100 = 1.6 g, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce dressing (~300 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be mindful of the soy sauce component, which contributes approximately 300 mg sodium per 100 g; requesting reduced soy sauce or a smaller portion can help limit sodium intake to within recommended daily limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~15) and low glycemic load per serving (~1.6 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Spinach with Beijing sesame sauce has a low glycemic index of approximately 15 and a glycemic load of approximately 1.6 per 150 g serving, making it a suitable vegetable dish for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, though the caloric density from sesame paste should be accounted for in overall meal planning.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Predominantly unsaturated fats from sesame paste; rich in potassium, folate, and vitamin K from spinach
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The sesame paste dressing provides predominantly monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fatty acids (approximately 9.1 g per 100 g combined) with low saturated fat (~1.6 g), and the spinach base contributes potassium (~420 mg) and folate (~110 mcg DFE), supporting cardiovascular health; sodium from soy sauce should be moderated.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spinach with Beijing Sesame Sauce (老北京麻酱菠菜, Lǎo Běijīng Májiàng Bōcài) is a classic cold appetiser from Beijing cuisine, widely served across northern Chinese restaurants. The dish is prepared by briefly blanching fresh spinach in boiling water, then immediately chilling it in cold water to preserve its vibrant green colour and crisp-tender texture. The cooled spinach is then dressed with a rich, nutty sauce made from Chinese sesame paste (zhima jiang / 芝麻酱), soy sauce, sesame oil, minced garlic, a touch of vinegar, and a small amount of sugar. The result is a refreshing, flavourful cold salad with a creamy, aromatic sesame coating. Nutritionally, the dish is a good source of plant-based protein (~5.9 g per 100 g), predominantly unsaturated fats from sesame paste (~9.1 g MUFA+PUFA per 100 g), dietary fibre (~2.1 g), and micronutrients including vitamin A (~280 mcg RAE), folate (~110 mcg DFE), iron (~2.8 mg), calcium (~110 mg), and potassium (~420 mg). The glycemic index is low (~15), making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content (~300 mg per 100 g) from soy sauce warrants attention for those with hypertension. The dish is vegetarian, vegan, and dairy-free, and contains sesame and soy allergens. It is commonly served as a starter or side dish in Beijing-style Chinese restaurants in Singapore and globally.

How many calories in 老北京麻酱菠菜?

Is Beijing sesame spinach high in sodium?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for 老北京麻酱菠菜 was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values were estimated by proportional combination of USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked spinach (#11458) and sesame paste (#12166), weighted for a typical recipe composition (~80% spinach, ~20% sesame dressing). GI was estimated by analogy from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked spinach. Confidence is medium due to recipe-level estimation rather than direct laboratory analysis.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy proportional composition (cooked spinach ~80%, sesame paste dressing ~20%) as no direct database entry exists for this dish. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as sesame paste and spinach contain no industrially produced trans fats; trace natural trans fats are below detection threshold. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is an entirely plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as neither spinach nor sesame paste contains meaningful vitamin D. 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a plant-based dish. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g (4.2), polyunsaturated_fat_g (4.9), potassium_mg (420.0), calcium_mg (110.0), iron_mg (2.8), vitamin_c_mg (8.0), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (280.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.09), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.12), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (1.0), vitamin_b6_mg (0.18), folate_mcg_dfe (110.0), vitamin_e_mg (1.8), phosphorus_mg (130.0), magnesium_mg (65.0), zinc_mg (0.9), selenium_mcg (3.5), water_g (72.0) are all estimated from USDA SR Legacy weighted composition. 7. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on cooked spinach GI from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found. 8. Sodium (~300 mg per 100 g) is estimated from typical soy sauce contribution in Beijing-style sesame dressing. 9. The dish is served at See Ya Tonight (今晚有局) restaurant in Singapore at SGD 20.80 per portion. 10. Allergens: sesame and soy are present; gluten may be present from soy sauce (unless tamari is used).
